problem_statement.Title: CalibratedClassifierCV fails under set_config(transform_output="pandas") due to IsotonicRegression.predict returning 2D/pandas output
problem_statement.Description: When global set_config(transform_output="pandas") is active, CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') can crash during probability prediction because IsotonicRegression.predict delegates to transform() and may return a 2D/pandas container (shape (n_samples, 1)) instead of a 1D numpy.ndarray; this breaks the assignment in _CalibratedClassifier.predict_proba which expects a 1D array per class.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sklearn/isotonic.py::IsotonicRegression.predict — incorrect assumption: predict() returns a 1D numpy.ndarray. Implementation currently does "return self.transform(T)" (isotonic.py:413). Because IsotonicRegression implements get_feature_names_out and TransformerMixin/_SetOutputMixin auto-wraps transform, transform() can be wrapped to return a pandas DataFrame (dense pandas container) when set_config(transform_output="pandas") is active. The wrapped transform therefore can yield a 2D container (n_samples, 1) rather than a 1D ndarray. Location: sklearn/isotonic.py::IsotonicRegression.predict and transform.

2) sklearn/calibration.py::_CalibratedClassifier.predict_proba — incorrect assumption: calibrator.predict(this_pred) yields a 1D array that can be assigned into a 1D slice proba[:, class_idx]. The code performs `proba[:, class_idx] = calibrator.predict(this_pred)` (calibration.py:755-763), which requires the right-hand side to have shape (n_samples,). If calibrator.predict returns a 2D array or single-column pandas DataFrame (shape (n_samples, 1)), a broadcasting ValueError occurs. Location: sklearn/calibration.py::_CalibratedClassifier.predict_proba.

3) sklearn/utils/_set_output.py::_SetOutputMixin and base.TransformerMixin — relevant behavior: the auto-wrapping logic targets transform and fit_transform only (not predict), and the autowrap activates when an estimator implements get_feature_names_out. Because IsotonicRegression defines get_feature_names_out and transform is defined on the class, _SetOutputMixin wraps transform to return a pandas DataFrame when the global configuration set_output for transform is set to "pandas". Location: sklearn/utils/_set_output.py and sklearn/base.py::TransformerMixin.

4) sklearn/utils/validation.column_or_1d — available utility: column_or_1d coerces column vectors or single-column pandas containers into a 1D numpy.ndarray of shape (n_samples,), and is the appropriate normalization to satisfy predict's public contract (a 1D ndarray). Location: sklearn/utils/validation.py::column_or_1d. The observed symptom (ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (20,1) into shape (20,)) follows from items 1 and 2 when IsotonicRegression.predict returns a 2D/pandas output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: CalibratedClassifierCV(method='isotonic') should produce probability predictions or class predictions without raising an exception when set_config(transform_output="pandas") is active. predict_proba/predict should return outputs with shapes appropriate for the estimator (probability matrix shape (n_samples, n_classes), and per-class calibrator.predict calls should yield 1D arrays of shape (n_samples,)).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling predict/predict_proba raises a ValueError during assignment in _CalibratedClassifier.predict_proba: broadcasting fails because calibrator.predict(...) returned a (n_samples, 1) array or pandas/DataFrame instead of a 1D array, resulting in error: "could not broadcast input array from shape (20,1) into shape (20,)".

problem_statement.Title: ColumnTransformer with pandas output fails when a transformer selects 0 features (empty selection + 'passthrough')
problem_statement.Description: When ColumnTransformer is used with set_output(transform="pandas"), a transformer step that uses 'passthrough' but selects 0 columns (empty column selection) can cause a pandas ValueError about column-length mismatch while assigning DataFrame.columns. The failure occurs during fit/transform of a Pipeline containing such a ColumnTransformer (user example: a ('categorical', 'passthrough', []) step together with another transformer, with pipeline.set_output(transform='pandas')).
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple code locations in sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py handle the string specifier 'passthrough' and empty column selections in an order that violates the implicit assumption (expressed in comments and consumers) that iterators over transformers only yield non-empty selections. The verified findings are: 

1) ColumnTransformer._iter (replace_strings=True, unfitted path) replaces trans == "passthrough" with a newly created FunctionTransformer before checking _is_empty_column_selection(columns). (See _iter: replace of 'passthrough' occurs prior to the emptiness check; this causes a ('name', 'passthrough', []) spec to be converted into a FunctionTransformer and yielded.)

2) ColumnTransformer._update_fitted_transformers checks old == "passthrough" and consumes next(fitted_transformers) before checking _is_empty_column_selection(column). (As written, the code treats the 'passthrough' spec as a consumer of a fitted transformer and stores the fitted passthrough in self._name_to_fitted_passthrough.)

3) ColumnTransformer._hstack (pandas output path) and _add_prefix_for_feature_names_out assume the sequence of transformer names produced by self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True) aligns 1:1 and in order with the list of transformed outputs Xs. _hstack uses transformer_names = [t[0] for t in self._iter(fitted=True, replace_strings=True)] and feature_names_outs = [X.columns for X in Xs] and then sets output.columns based on pairing these lists. If the pairing is inconsistent (different lengths/orders), assigning output.columns raises the observed pandas ValueError (Length mismatch).

4) The helper _is_empty_column_selection(column) exists and correctly detects empty selections (empty list or all-False boolean array), but in the key code paths above it is consulted after a special-case handling of 'passthrough'. Because 'passthrough' is replaced/consumed before emptiness is checked, a 'passthrough' spec with an empty selection is treated as a real transformer rather than being skipped.

5) The code contains a comment/assumption (in _record_output_indices) that `_iter` only generates transformers that have a non empty selection, but the ordering in _iter (see #1) permits passthrough-with-empty to be yielded. This contradiction between intended/assumed behavior (comments/consumers) and actual ordering is the root cause of the mismatch that can lead to the pandas Length mismatch error when set_output(transform='pandas') is used.

In short: the diagnostic root cause is the ordering of operations around replacing/consuming the 'passthrough' spec and skipping empty column selections. That ordering causes passthrough-with-empty to be treated as a yielded/consumed transformer in some code paths even though other parts of the code (and comments) assume empty selections are skipped; that broken assumption about which transformers are present/produced leads to the observed name/column-length mismatch.
StepsToReproduce: Create a pandas DataFrame X with columns e.g. ['a','b','c','d'] and target y.

Create a ColumnTransformer with steps: ('categorical', 'passthrough', categorical_features) and ('numerical', Pipeline([('scaler', RobustScaler()), ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median'))]), numerical_features), remainder='drop'.
Place the ColumnTransformer inside a Pipeline with a classifier (e.g. LGBMClassifier) and call .set_output(transform='pandas') on the Pipeline.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Fitting the pipeline succeeds. Any ColumnTransformer step that selects 0 features is effectively ignored for naming purposes (it should not contribute columns or feature names), and the resulting pandas output has DataFrame.columns that match the actual transformed features.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pipeline.fit(...) can raise a pandas ValueError during assignment of DataFrame.columns: "Length mismatch: Expected axis has <N> elements, new values have <M> elements". This happens when a ColumnTransformer contains a ('...', 'passthrough', []) step and the pipeline output is configured to return pandas objects (set_output(transform='pandas')).

problem_statement.Title: unique_labels rejects pandas nullable (extension) dtypes (Int64/Float64/boolean)
problem_statement.Description: When a pandas Series uses nullable extension dtypes (e.g. 'Int64', 'Float64', 'boolean'), sklearn.utils.multiclass.unique_labels can raise ValueError("Mix type of y not allowed, got types {...}") because those Series are classified as 'unknown' rather than numeric/binary.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) unique_labels aggregates the target-type classification returned by type_of_target for each input and raises when the set of detected types contains more than one element. (See sklearn/utils/multiclass.py: unique_labels: "ys_types = set(type_of_target(x) for x in ys)" and "if len(ys_types) > 1: raise ValueError('Mix type of y not allowed, got types %s' % ys_types)".)

2) type_of_target and its helper is_multilabel use the array-conversion path (xp.asarray / y.__array__) without applying the pandas-extension early-conversion pattern used elsewhere. In particular, is_multilabel converts inputs with xp.asarray (falling back to dtype=object) and type_of_target later treats non-sparse object-dtype arrays (whose first flat element is not str) as 'unknown' (sklearn/utils/multiclass.py: is_multilabel xp.asarray path; type_of_target lines where it returns 'unknown' for object dtype). This means pandas nullable (extension) dtypes can become object-backed arrays and be classified as 'unknown' instead of 'binary'/'multiclass'.

3) The codebase already contains a canonical, guarded detection/conversion pattern for pandas extension dtypes in sklearn/utils/validation.py: _pandas_dtype_needs_early_conversion detects extension dtypes that require early conversion (imports from pandas.api.types and returns True for certain extension dtypes), and check_array uses this to perform an early astype conversion for pandas inputs. (See sklearn/utils/validation.py: definition of _pandas_dtype_needs_early_conversion and the pandas_requires_conversion / array.astype(new_dtype) block in check_array.) The absence of an analogous early-conversion step in type_of_target/is_multilabel is what allows extension-dtype Series to be misclassified.

4) Multiple call sites are affected by the same missing-handling pattern: unique_labels is a concrete user-facing symptom, but other callers of type_of_target (including check_classification_targets and any code that relies on type_of_target) will see the same misclassification if passed pandas nullable extension dtypes. Also, because is_multilabel performs xp.asarray early and returns quickly for multilabel formats, failing to handle extension dtypes there can cause misclassification before further checks run.

5) Tests that explicitly verify unique_labels (and related type_of_target behavior) accept pandas nullable (extension) dtypes are missing. The existing test suite includes many tests for unique_labels and for check_array conversion behavior for pandas extension dtypes, but there is no test asserting that unique_labels accepts 'Int64'/'Float64'/'boolean' Series without raising the "Mix type of y not allowed" error. (See sklearn/utils/tests/test_multiclass.py where unique_labels is tested over many formats, but not over pandas nullable dtypes.)
StepsToReproduce: Run the following in Python (or as a pytest test):
unique_labels(y_true, y_predicted)  # raises ValueError: Mix type of y not allowed, got types {'binary', 'unknown'}
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When pandas nullable extension dtypes ('Int64', 'Float64', 'boolean') are passed to unique_labels (together with compatible other inputs), unique_labels should not raise a "Mix type of y not allowed" error and should return the combined set of unique labels, matching the behavior observed when numpy-backed dtypes (int64/float64/bool) are used.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: unique_labels raises ValueError: "Mix type of y not allowed, got types {'binary', 'unknown'}" when y_true is a pandas Series with dtype 'Int64', 'Float64', or 'boolean' and y_predicted is a NumPy-backed int64 Series.

problem_statement.Title: FeatureUnion + set_config(transform_output='pandas') raises ValueError for aggregating transformers
problem_statement.Description: When set_config(transform_output='pandas') is enabled and a FeatureUnion contains a transformer that aggregates or otherwise returns fewer rows than the original input (e.g., groupby.sum), calling fit_transform raises a pandas ValueError. The exception is triggered during the output-wrapping step that applies the original input's index onto the transformer's pandas output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sklearn/utils/_set_output.py::_wrap_in_pandas_container unconditionally assigns the provided index onto DataFrame outputs (code path: if isinstance(data_to_wrap, pd.DataFrame): ... if index is not None: data_to_wrap.index = index). This encodes the incorrect assumption that a transformer's pandas DataFrame output always has the same number of rows as the original input.

2) sklearn/utils/_set_output.py:_wrap_data_with_container and _wrap_method_output (the decorator used by _SetOutputMixin) pass the original input's index into _wrap_in_pandas_container via index=getattr(original_input, "index", None). The decorator uses the method's input X as original_input, so the original input index is always presented to the pandas wrapper when auto-wrapping transform/fit_transform outputs.

3) sklearn/pipeline.py:FeatureUnion._hstack, when transform_output is configured as "pandas" and transformer outputs expose .iloc, concatenates transformer outputs with pd.concat(Xs, axis=1). If one or more transformers produce aggregated outputs with fewer rows than the original input (for example, a pandas Series/DataFrame with a shorter index), the pd.concat result has the transformer's index and a different row-count than the original input.

4) The combination of (1)-(3) produces the failure surface: the wrapper receives a DataFrame whose row-count equals the transform outputs but then unconditionally attempts to set the original input index (which can have a different length). The repro stack trace shows concrete values consistent with this: transformer output length 4 vs original input index length 96 (pandas error: "Length mismatch: Expected axis has 4 elements, new values have 96 elements").

5) Series/other non-DataFrame outputs are part of the same pattern: FeatureUnion can accept Series outputs from transformers (e.g., groupby.sum returns a Series), pd.concat(Xs, axis=1) converts/combines them into a DataFrame with the transformer's index, and the unguarded assignment in _wrap_in_pandas_container still attempts to set the original input index on that DataFrame.

6) The repository's tests for set_output (sklearn/utils/tests/test_set_output.py and the generic set_output checks used in test_common) exercise column/index overrides for DataFrame wrapping but do not include a regression test that reproduces an aggregating transformer inside a FeatureUnion with transform_output='pandas'. This test gap means the behavior where outputs have different row-counts is not covered by existing tests.
StepsToReproduce: Create a pandas DataFrame with an index of length 96 (e.g., hourly range) and a column 'value', plus a 'date' column with fewer unique values (e.g., 4 unique dates).
Define a transformer whose transform returns an aggregated Series/DataFrame, e.g., X['value'].groupby(X['date']).sum().
Observe the ValueError thrown during output wrapping.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No exception is raised when set_config(transform_output='pandas') is used. If a transformer returns an output whose number of rows differs from the original input (e.g., due to aggregation or filtering), the wrapper should not forcibly apply the original input index to that output. The user-visible expectation is that the union's fit_transform returns a pandas DataFrame whose row-count and index reflect the transformer outputs (for aggregated outputs, the aggregated row-count and corresponding index).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A pandas ValueError is raised during output wrapping: "Length mismatch: Expected axis has 4 elements, new values have 96 elements". The error occurs because the pandas wrapper attempted to set the original input index (length 96) on a DataFrame produced by an aggregating transformer (length 4) while transforming via FeatureUnion with transform_output='pandas'.

hints_text:
As noted in the [glossery](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/glossary.html#term-transform), Scikit-learn transformers expects that `transform`'s output have the same number of samples as the input. This exception is held in `FeatureUnion` when processing data and tries to make sure that the output index is the same as the input index. In principle, we can have a less restrictive requirement and only set the index if it is not defined.

To better understand your use case, how do you intend to use the `FeatureUnion` in the overall pipeline?


> Scikit-learn transformers expects that transform's output have the same number of samples as the input

I haven't known that. Good to know. What is the correct way to aggregate or drop rows in a pipeline? Isn't that supported?

> To better understand your use case, how do you intend to use the FeatureUnion in the overall pipeline?

The actual use case: I have a time series (`price`) with hourly frequency. It is a single series with a datetime index. I have built a dataframe with pipeline and custom transformers (by also violating the rule to have same number of inputs and outputs) which aggregates the data (calculates daily mean, and some moving average of daily means) then I have transformed back to hourly frequency (using same values for all the hours of a day). So the dataframe has (`date`, `price`, `mean`, `moving_avg`) columns at that point with hourly frequency ("same number input/output" rule violated again). After that I have added the problematic `FeatureUnion`. One part of the union simply drops `price` and "collapses" the remaining part to daily data (as I said all the remaining columns has the same values on the same day). On the other part of the feature union I calculate a standard devition between `price` and `moving_avg` on daily basis. So I have the (`date`, `mean`, `moving_avg`) on the left side of the feature union and an `std` on the right side. Both have daily frequency. I would like to have a dataframe with (`date`, `mean`, `moving_avg`, `std`) at the end of the transformation.
As I see there is the same "problem" in `ColumnTransfromer`.
I have a look at how `scikit-learn` encapsulates output into a `DataFrame` and found this code block:

https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/main/sklearn/utils/_set_output.py#L55-L62

Is there any reason to set index here? If transformer returned a `DataFrame` this already has some kind of index. Why should we restore the original input index? What is the use case when a transformer changes the `DataFrame`'s index and `scikit-learn` has to restore it automatically to the input index?

With index restoration it is also expected for transformers that index should not be changed (or if it is changed by transformer then `scikit-learn` restores the original one which could be a bit unintuitive). Is this an intended behaviour?

What is the design decision to not allow changing index and row count in data by transformers? In time series problems I think it is very common to aggregate raw data and modify original index.
